next_steps: Retain the title-page imprint and dated foreword separately.
Consult Chaucer Society issue records or a copy-specific accession record before collapsing them into one publication date.
openQuestions.question: Should this carrier be cited strictly as 1885, as 1886, or as an 1885 imprint with an 1886-dated foreword?

next_steps: Ask Internet Archive or Google Books for source-copy metadata tied to Google Books ID PEHGaZd-ka0C.
Treat the visible Stanford Library mark as suggestive carrier evidence, not a resolved provenance statement.
openQuestions.question: Which institutional copy was digitized as harleianmschauc00chaugoog?

next_steps: Request written clarification from U-M Digital Collections.
Until clarified, preserve both statements and do not interpret NoC-US as a redistribution license.
openQuestions.question: Does U-M's current No Copyright-United States statement supersede the older restriction language embedded in the 2006 XML header?

next_steps: Monitor the British Library catalog and digitized-manuscripts replacement services after service restoration.
If direct image evidence is required, request the exact folios through the British Library reproduction route subject to condition and fees.
Keep the complete Norman Blake Harley transcription as a supporting encoding, not a substitute facsimile.
openQuestions.question: Can complete public facsimiles of Harley MS 7334, Royal MS 17 D XV f. 284r, or Arundel MS 248 f. 154r be obtained from a current institutional service?

reviewNotes: Harley order is witness evidence, not a reconstructed authorial final order.
No source-supply edge is asserted for the missing Squire-Franklin material: Furnivall prints the surviving Harley sequence without filling the loss.
The issued woodcut suites are established only for the audited 1885 Harley carrier; no inference is made about earlier Corpus or Lansdowne part carriers.

lineage.summary: The repository XML copies U-M/CME encoding AGZ8246.0001.001, an uncorrected OCR and Level-1 structural representation of Furnivall's 1885 one-manuscript Harley MS 7334 edition. Furnivall directly transcribed Harley for the main Canterbury Tales and Gamelyn text, deliberately left an eight-leaf Group F loss unsupplied, retained Tyrwhitt's paragraph divisions for Melibee and the Parson's Tale, and used the Chaucer Society Six-Text only for comparison and navigation. The edition-defined whole separately excerpts a 15-line Nun's Priest end-link from Royal MS 17 D XV, reproduces the bilingual Angelus ad Virginem from Arundel MS 248, and includes W. H. Hooper's 23 Ellesmere-derived and nine Gg.4.27-derived design-bearing plate surfaces. The two complete Internet Archive/Google scans are distinct facsimile carriers whose different title-leaf and repeated-scan states are preserved rather than conflated.

catalogSummary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer; anonymous responsibility for the non-Chaucerian Tale of Gamelyn and Angelus ad Virginem appendix
catalogSummary.date: Canterbury Tales composed chiefly in the late 1380s and 1390s; Harley MS 7334 c. 1405-1415; source edition 1885
catalogSummary.region: England; Harley witness probably London or East Anglia
catalogSummary.title: The Canterbury Tales

catalogingSubject.label: The Canterbury Tales (Harley MS 7334/Furnivall representation)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Geoffrey Chaucer's unfinished Middle English pilgrimage frame and conventional twenty-four-tale collection in the order and readings of British Library Harley MS 7334, as edited by Frederick James Furnivall in 1885 and represented by U-M/CME item AGZ8246.0001.001. The boundary also exposes, but does not count as additional Canterbury tales, the anonymous non-Chaucerian Tale of Gamelyn transmitted after the Cook's Tale; prologues, links, and the Retraction; a separately sourced Royal MS 17 D XV Nun's Priest end-link; the bilingual Angelus ad Virginem from Arundel MS 248; and two W. H. Hooper woodcut suites copied from Ellesmere MS EL 26 C 9 and Cambridge MS Gg.4.27. The abstract work, manuscript-specific readings, printed edition, page-image carriers, digital encodings, repository XML, and appendix sources remain distinct lineage layers.

contentParts.label: Group A, Fragment I
contentParts.summary: General Prologue; Knight, Miller, Reeve, incomplete Cook, then the separately anonymous Gamelyn in Harley order.

contentParts.label: General Prologue
contentParts.summary: Pilgrimage frame, pilgrim portraits, and tale-telling agreement.

contentParts.label: The Knight's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse romance of Palamon, Arcite, and Emelye.

contentParts.label: Miller's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Miller interrupts the ordered tale sequence and introduces his fabliau.

contentParts.label: The Miller's Tale
contentParts.summary: Fabliau of Nicholas, Alison, John, and Absolon.

contentParts.label: Reeve's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Reeve answers the Miller and introduces his retaliatory tale.

contentParts.label: The Reeve's Tale
contentParts.summary: Retaliatory fabliau of two clerks and a miller.

contentParts.label: Cook's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Cook reacts to the Reeve's tale and introduces his own.

contentParts.label: The Cook's Tale
contentParts.summary: Incomplete fabliau of Perkyn Revel.

contentParts.label: The Tale of Gamelyn
contentParts.summary: Anonymous 902-line Middle English outlaw romance transmitted after the incomplete Cook's Tale and explicitly labeled a spurious appendix by Furnivall. It is not Chaucerian and does not increment the conventional twenty-four-tale Canterbury Tales count.

contentParts.label: Group B-alpha, Fragment II
contentParts.summary: Man of Law sequence.

contentParts.label: Man of Law's Head-link and Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Host calls on the Man of Law, whose prologue precedes the Constance narrative.

contentParts.label: The Man of Law's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse tale of Constance.

contentParts.label: Group D, Fragment V
contentParts.summary: Wife of Bath, Friar, and Summoner sequence.

contentParts.label: Wife of Bath's Preamble and Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Wife's autobiographical marriage argument, including the Friar-Summoner wrangle before her tale.

contentParts.label: The Wife of Bath's Tale
contentParts.summary: Arthurian romance of marriage and sovereignty.

contentParts.label: Friar's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Friar introduces his attack on summoners.

contentParts.label: The Friar's Tale
contentParts.summary: Clerical satire of a corrupt summoner and a devil.

contentParts.label: Summoner's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Summoner answers the Friar and introduces his anti-fraternal tale.

contentParts.label: The Summoner's Tale
contentParts.summary: Anti-fraternal fabliau.

contentParts.label: Group E, Fragment VI
contentParts.summary: Clerk and Merchant sequence.

contentParts.label: Clerk's Head-link and Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Host calls on the Clerk, whose prologue introduces Griselda.

contentParts.label: The Clerk's Tale
contentParts.summary: Six-part verse tale of Griselda with Chaucer's envoy.

contentParts.label: Merchant's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Merchant responds to the Clerk's marriage tale.

contentParts.label: The Merchant's Tale
contentParts.summary: Marriage tale with fabliau elements.

contentParts.label: Merchant-Squire Link
contentParts.summary: The Host turns from the Merchant to the Squire.

contentParts.label: Group F, Fragment VII
contentParts.summary: Squire and Franklin sequence interrupted by an eight-leaf manuscript loss.

contentParts.label: Squire's Head-link
contentParts.summary: The Squire begins his turn after the preceding Merchant-Squire link.

contentParts.label: The Squire's Tale
contentParts.summary: Incomplete romance; the Harley text stops before an eight-leaf manuscript loss.

contentParts.label: The Franklin's Tale

locators.value: resumes at f. 157v; f. 157r and seven preceding leaves lost

contentParts.summary: Only the surviving tail is present; the Squire-Franklin link and Franklin lines 1-487 are absent and not supplied.

contentParts.label: Group G, Fragment VIII
contentParts.summary: Second Nun and Canon's Yeoman sequence.

contentParts.label: The Second Nun's Tale
contentParts.summary: Saint's life of Cecilia with prologue.

contentParts.label: Canon's Yeoman Prologue, Opening
contentParts.summary: Opening portion of the Canon's Yeoman's arrival and introduction.

contentParts.label: Canon's Yeoman Preamble, Continuation
contentParts.summary: Continuation of the Canon's Yeoman's preamble before the alchemical tale.

contentParts.label: The Canon's Yeoman's Tale
contentParts.summary: Alchemy and fraud narrative.

contentParts.label: Group C, Fragment IV
contentParts.summary: Physician and Pardoner sequence.

contentParts.label: The Physician's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse tale of Virginia.

contentParts.label: Physician-Pardoner Introduction
contentParts.summary: The Host responds to the Physician and calls on the Pardoner.

contentParts.label: Pardoner's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Pardoner exposes his preaching methods and avarice.

contentParts.label: The Pardoner's Tale
contentParts.summary: Exemplum of three rioters seeking Death.

contentParts.label: Group B-beta, Fragment III
contentParts.summary: Shipman through Nun's Priest sequence.

contentParts.label: The Shipman's Tale
contentParts.summary: Fabliau of a merchant, wife, and monk.

contentParts.label: Host-Prioress Link
contentParts.summary: The Host calls on the Prioress after the Shipman's tale.

contentParts.label: The Prioress's Tale
contentParts.summary: Miracle-of-the-Virgin narrative.

contentParts.label: Prioress-Thopas Link
contentParts.summary: The Host turns from the Prioress to Chaucer the pilgrim.

contentParts.label: The Tale of Sir Thopas
contentParts.summary: Parodic romance interrupted by the Host.

contentParts.label: Thopas-Melibee Interruption and Introduction
contentParts.summary: The Host stops Sir Thopas and Chaucer introduces Melibee.

contentParts.label: The Tale of Melibee
contentParts.summary: Prose moral tale; Furnivall retains Tyrwhitt's paragraph breaks because Harley supplies neither lineation nor paragraphing.

contentParts.label: Monk's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Host's response to Melibee and the Monk's introduction.

contentParts.label: The Monk's Tale
contentParts.summary: Series of tragedies.

contentParts.label: Monk-Nun's Priest Link
contentParts.summary: The Monk is interrupted and the Nun's Priest is called to tell a merry tale.

contentParts.label: The Nun's Priest's Tale
contentParts.summary: Beast fable of Chauntecleer and Pertelote; Harley lacks the separately appended end-link.

contentParts.label: Group H, Fragment IX
contentParts.summary: Manciple sequence.

contentParts.label: Manciple's Prologue
contentParts.summary: The Manciple mocks the Cook and introduces his tale.

contentParts.label: The Manciple's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse tale of Apollo and the crow.

contentParts.label: Group I, Fragment X
contentParts.summary: Parson sequence and Retraction; the printed analytical contents are represented only by an omitted XML division.

contentParts.label: Blank-Parson Link
contentParts.summary: The Host calls on the Parson for the final tale.

contentParts.label: Contents and Analysis of the Parson's Tale

locators.value: AGZ8246.0001.001:12.2; PB 607-611; no encoded P, L, or MILESTONE content

contentParts.summary: Printed analytical contents on pp. 593-597, represented by an empty XML division explicitly marked omitted.

contentParts.label: The Parson's Tale
contentParts.summary: Prose penitential treatise; Furnivall retains Tyrwhitt's paragraphing.

contentParts.label: Chaucer's Retraction
contentParts.summary: Closing retraction after the Parson's Tale.

contentParts.label: Nun's Priest's End-link appendix
contentParts.summary: Fifteen-line end-link printed from Royal MS 17 D XV, f. 284r, explicitly described as absent from Harley MS 7334.

contentParts.label: Angelus ad Virginem appendix

contentParts.summary: William Harcourt Hooper's 23 copied-and-cut teller portraits, in carrier order and captions: The Knight, The Miller, The Reeve, The Cook, The Man of Law, The Shipman, The Prioress, Chaucer, The Monk, The Nun's Priest, The Doctor, The Pardoner, The Wife of Bath, The Friar, The Summoner, The Clerk of Oxford, The Merchant, The Squire, The Franklin, The Second Nun, The Canon's Yeoman, The Manciple, and The Parson. They are visual apparatus copied from Ellesmere MS EL 26 C 9, not Harley textual evidence.

contentParts.summary: William Harcourt Hooper's copies of 12 figures on nine design-bearing plate surfaces: six teller surfaces captioned The Reeve, The Cook of London, The Wife of Bath, The Pardoner, The Monk, and The Manciple, followed by three paired allegory surfaces captioned Envy and Charity, Gluttony and Abstinence, and Lechery and Chastity. These are visual apparatus from Cambridge MS Gg.4.27, not Harley textual evidence.

dateStatements.display: London imprint dated 1885; editor's foreword dated 8 January 1886
notes: This dates Furnivall's representation, not first publication of The Canterbury Tales. The normalized range preserves the discrepancy between title-page imprint and dated foreword without silently converting the imprint to 1886.

scope.description: The exact edition-defined representation and its separately sourced textual and visual appendices

extent.description: Twenty-four conventional Chaucerian tales are counted: twenty-two verse and two prose. The 706 LG and 16,772 L counts are semantic XML element counts after excluding Gamelyn's eight LG and 902 L elements; they still include verse prologues and links and are not a critical lineation. The 64-part human-readable inventory preserves all 49 main-text DIV2 boundaries, ten Harley-order group containers, a separately exposed Retraction within DIV2 12.3, and four edition appendices. Raw XML metrics are 13 DIV1, 49 DIV2, 41 DIV3, 714 LG, 17,674 L, 217 P, 1,103 MILESTONE, and 778 PB elements. Prologues, links, Retraction, edition appendices, and the non-Chaucerian Gamelyn do not increment the tale count.

formStatements.description: Predominantly verse, with the prose Tale of Melibee and Parson's Tale; Gamelyn is verse and does not change the mixed whole-work classification.

scope.description: The tale-telling journey and links across the Chaucerian fragments; excludes Gamelyn and edition appendices

scope.description: The medieval main text, including Gamelyn; excludes the Latin hymn column, modern editorial prose, and visual apparatus

notes: Harley order is recorded as witness evidence, not presented as Chaucer's final authorial order.
The two duplicated folio labels, omitted f. 157r marker, absent terminal 286b, and Six-Text milestone gaps are preserved as layer-specific source facts rather than silently normalized.
The XML's 778 page breaks inventory the historical scan carrier; omitted front/back divisions mean page coverage is broader than encoded textual content.

subjects.label: Religion, devotion, anticlerical satire, and penitence

summaries.value: Pilgrims traveling toward Canterbury tell stories in many forms and social voices. This record preserves the particular sequence and textual state of Harley MS 7334, including the non-Chaucerian Gamelyn after the incomplete Cook's Tale and an eight-leaf loss that removes the Squire-Franklin transition and the opening 487 Franklin lines. Its edition-defined whole also includes a Royal MS 17 D XV end-link, the bilingual Arundel MS 248 Angelus ad Virginem, and issued Ellesmere and Gg.4.27 woodcut suites, each bound to its separate source rather than attributed to Harley.

Harley order: A/I, B-alpha/II, D/V, E/VI, F/VII, G/VIII, C/IV, B-beta/III, H/IX, I/X. The U-M XML transcribes twenty-four Chaucerian tales plus Gamelyn, omits broad front and back matter, represents the Parson analysis as an empty omitted division, and retains complete page, folio, and Six-Text milestone systems with documented source anomalies.

notes: The Harley MS 7334/Furnivall qualifier defines this cataloging representation; it is not part of the conventional work title.
Capitalization is normalized for display from the edition and U-M bibliographic title.

work.summary: Pilgrims traveling toward Canterbury tell stories in many forms and social voices. This record preserves the particular sequence and textual state of Harley MS 7334, including the non-Chaucerian Gamelyn after the incomplete Cook's Tale and an eight-leaf loss that removes the Squire-Franklin transition and the opening 487 Franklin lines. Its edition-defined whole also includes a Royal MS 17 D XV end-link, the bilingual Arundel MS 248 Angelus ad Virginem, and issued Ellesmere and Gg.4.27 woodcut suites, each bound to its separate source rather than attributed to Harley.
